segments.purgatorio-23-g16: This segment is a single punctuation mark (a comma) serving to separate the preceding speech from the speaker's report; it has no lexical content to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-23-g17: comincia’ io is a poetic contraction: comincia’ stands for cominciai (passato remoto, first person singular) or a contracted past form meaning "I began/started," and io is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I' placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-23-g18: ed elli: ed is the clitic form of e, 'and'; elli here is an archaic subject pronoun meaning 'he' (introducing the words of another speaker), but the provided sense list does not include that meaning.
segments.purgatorio-23-g19: This is the reported speech. "Ombre" is the vocative/nominal subject 'shades' (ghosts); "che" is the relative 'who/that'; "vanno" is present indicative 'go/proceed'; "forse" is the adverb 'perhaps'; "di lor dover" = 'of their duty' (di + lor = 'of their'); "solvendo" is a gerund from solvere, here 'loosening/solving' (literally 'untying' or 'resolving') modifying the action; "il nodo" = 'the knot' (a metaphorical 'knot' of obligation). Word order places gerund after the noun-phrase it modifies (their duty) and uses poetic economy of clauses.
segments.purgatorio-23-g20: Sì come is a fixed comparative phrase meaning 'just as' or 'as'; i peregrin pensosi is a noun phrase with the archaic peregrin ('pilgrims') and the adjective pensosi 'thoughtful' placed after the noun (typical of poetic word order); fanno is present indicative plural 'they do' used generically to describe habitual action.
segments.purgatorio-23-g21: giugnendo is a gerund of giugnere (arriving/coming) indicating contemporaneous action ('as they arrive'); per cammin = 'along the road' (per + noun expresses movement along a path); gente non nota = 'people not known' (non nota negates nota, 'known' — note gender/agreement is poetic).
segments.purgatorio-23-g22: che is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing the relative clause; si volgono is a reflexive present (volgere → si volgono) meaning 'turn themselves' → 'turn to/face'; ad essa = 'to it/her' (essa refers forward to that person/group); e non restanno = 'and do not remain/stay' (non + present indicative restanno).
segments.purgatorio-23-g23: così = 'so/thus' linking the simile; di retro a noi literally 'from behind us' or 'behind us' (di + retro gives 'from the rear'); the prepositional phrase locates the following action relative to the narrator.
segments.purgatorio-23-g24: più tosto is a comparative/adverbial phrase meaning 'rather' or 'more quickly'; mota is an adjective/past participle 'moved' (feminine) agreeing with the upcoming feminine noun 'turba' (crowd), so the attribute is anticipatory: the crowd was more quickly moved/coming.
segments.purgatorio-23-g25: venendo e trapassando are gerunds 'coming and passing by' describing how the subject acted; ci is the indirect/object pronoun 'to/at us' or direct 'us' depending on the verb; ammirava is imperfect singular 'it marveled/admired' (agreement with the singular collective subject 'turba'); d’anime is a contraction of di + anime 'of souls'; turba here means 'crowd' (a feminine collective noun, hence the agreement mota); tacita e devota are adjectives 'silent and devout' describing the turba.
segments.purgatorio-23-g26: Ne li occhi = 'in the eyes' (ne used adverbially/locatively + the old article li); era is imperfect 'was' describing state; ciascuna 'each one' is the subject (each person/face); oscura e cava are predicative adjectives 'dark and hollow.' The syntax places the locative frontally for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-23-g27: palida ne la faccia = 'pale in the face'; palida is an adjective agreeing with ciascuna (feminine); ne + la faccia repeats the locative 'in the face' (poetic variation in prepositional expression).
segments.purgatorio-23-g28: e tanto scema che ... is a result clause: e tanto scema ('and so diminished/thin') introduces che with a consequence; da l’ossa la pelle s’informava literally 'the skin took shape from the bones' — s’informava is reflexive imperfect of informare meaning 'to take shape/form' (here describing the skin clinging to the bones).
segments.purgatorio-23-g29: Non credo che introduces a subordinate clause requiring the subjunctive: fosse fatto secco is a passive periphrasis in the imperfect subjunctive (fosse) plus past participle (fatto) meaning 'were made' or 'had become' dry; così a buccia strema is a poetic locution 'thus to the last skin' (i.e., 'so down to the skin'); Erisittone is the proper name (Erysichthon of myth).
segments.purgatorio-23-g30: per digiunar is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by fasting' or 'because of fasting' (per + infinitive expresses cause or means).
segments.purgatorio-23-g31: quando più n’ebbe tema: quando = 'when'; più = 'most' (to the greatest degree); n’ is the clitic form of ne meaning 'of it' or 'about it' (here 'the fear of it'); ebbe is passato remoto 'he had/felt'; tema = 'fear' — together 'when he most feared it.'
segments.purgatorio-23-g46: This segment is a single punctuation mark (a period) ending the previous dialogue or line. It has no lexical material to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-23-g47: "Mai non l’avrei riconosciuto al viso;" uses the conditional perfect (avrei + past participle riconosciuto) to express a hypothetical or counterfactual past: "I would have recognized." The clitic l’ (= lo) is the direct object "him/it" placed before the verb (standard in Italian). "Mai non" is an emphatic negative meaning "never," and "al viso" (a + il) indicates recognition by the face/appearance.
segments.purgatorio-23-g48: "ma ne la voce sua mi fu palese" — "ma" = but; "ne" here has a locative/partitive force meaning "in/within" (archaic order: ne + noun) so "ne la voce sua" = "in his voice." "Mi fu palese" is a past-tense construction (fu, perfective of essere) with the adjective palese as predicate: literally "it was manifest to me" = "it became clear to me."
segments.purgatorio-23-g49: "Questa favilla tutta mi raccese" — "Questa favilla tutta" = "this whole/entire spark;" "raccese" is the past tense of raccendere (to rekindle) with clitic "mi" showing the action affects the speaker: "rekindled in me." "mia conoscenza a la cangiata labbia" puts the possessed noun "mia conoscenza" (my recognition/memory) together with the phrase "a la cangiata labbia," an ablative/agentive phrase meaning "by/through the changed countenance" (a + la = 'to/by the'), so the spark revived his recognition prompted by the altered face.
segments.purgatorio-23-g50: "e ravvisai la faccia di Forese." — "e" = and; "ravvisai" is the first-person singular passato remoto (or simple past) meaning "I recognized/perceived"; the direct object is "la faccia" and "di Forese" identifies whose face it was. Word order is straightforward S-V-O with the proper name following di.
segments.purgatorio-23-g51: "Deh" is an interjection (archaic/expressive), equivalent to English "ah" or "please," used to introduce a plea or entreaty.
segments.purgatorio-23-g52: "non contendere a l’asciutta scabbia che mi scolora" — "non contendere" is a negative imperative (addressed to 'tu'): "do not contend/struggle/argue"; "a l’asciutta scabbia" uses a + definite article to name the cause/agent "the dry scab" (asciutta = dry, scabbia = scab/scales); "che mi scolora" is a relative clause "which discolours me" where "mi" is the indirect/dative-like clitic "me" and "scolora" is present tense "discolours/fades." The phrase means roughly "don't strain against the dry scab that makes me lose colour."
segments.purgatorio-23-g53: "pregava," imperfect of pregare, used narratively to mean "he begged/was begging/implored." It frames the preceding plea as an entreaty.
segments.purgatorio-23-g54: "la pelle," the direct mention of what the scab affects: "the skin." The article + noun is literal and emphatic in the appeal.
segments.purgatorio-23-g55: "né a difetto di carne ch’io abbia;" — "né" (nor) continues the negative plea; "a difetto di carne" literally "for lack of flesh/body" (a used idiomatically = "because of/for"); "ch’io abbia" uses the present subjunctive (abbia) after che to express a potential or hypothetical lack: "nor because of any lack of flesh that I might have."
segments.purgatorio-23-g56: "ma dimmi il ver di te," — "ma" = but; "dimmi" is the imperative "tell me" (di + mi fused to dimmi); "il ver di te" = "the truth about you" (ver = verità/true identity) — a direct command asking for truthful identification.
segments.purgatorio-23-g57: "dì chi son quelle due anime che là ti fanno scorta;" — "dì" (say/tell) imperative; "chi son quelle due anime" = "who those two souls are" (son = are, quelle due anime = those two souls); "che là ti fanno scorta" is a relative clause "that there escort you/keep you company" (fanno scorta = act as an escort). The whole clause requests identification of the companions.
segments.purgatorio-23-g58: "non rimaner che tu non mi favelle!»" — literally "do not remain so that you do not speak to me!" The idiom means "do not go away/leave me without speaking to me." "Non rimaner" is a negative imperative; the subordinate clause with "che" plus a negated verb (tu non mi favelle) expresses the undesired outcome to be avoided. "Favelle" is an archaic verb meaning "speak/talk."
segments.purgatorio-23-g86: This clause is a conditional protasis introduced by Se ("if"); prima ("before/previously") modifies the timing; fu is the simple past (passato remoto) of essere, giving a past-state "was"; la possa uses a substantive ("the power/ability") rather than a verb, followed by in te ("in you") to indicate possession or presence in the person; finita is a past participle used adjectivally/predicatively with fu ("had been finished/ended"); di peccar più is an infinitival complement (di + infinitive) specifying what the power to do was over: peccar (poetic infinitive for peccare) "to sin" and più "any more/longer." Word order is typical poetic Italian: article + noun (la possa) after the auxiliary, and the infinitive phrase follows finita to complete the idea.
segments.purgatorio-23-g87: This relative/consecutive clause begins with che (here a conjunction 'that'/'so that') and uses sovvenisse, the imperfect subjunctive of sovvenire, expressing potential/desired occurrence ('might occur'/'should occur'); l’ora (the hour/time) is the subject; del buon dolor ('of the good/salutary sorrow') specifies the kind of hour, and ch’a Dio ne rimarita is a subordinate relative clause describing that sorrow: ch’ = che 'which', a Dio 'to God', ne is a clitic with rimarita (here used with the sense 'in relation to it/them' or part of an idiomatic construction), and rimarita is a poetic perfect/passive-looking form (from rimaritare) meaning 'to be remitted/given back in marriage/remain attached again' — overall the clause says this sorrow might occur which effects a renewed relation to God.
segments.purgatorio-23-g88: This is the apodosis (main question) introduced by come (here 'how' or 'how is it that'); se tu is an emphatic subject pronoun ('you'), and qua sù ('here, up') marks location — qua (hither) + sù (upwards). Venuto is the past participle used absolutely in the sense of the perfect ('have come/arrived') with the implied auxiliary (common in poetry) and ancora ('again/still') modifies venuto to ask how he has come up once more. Word order places the manner/adverbials before the participle for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-23-g89: Io (I) is the subject; ti is the direct object 'you'; credea is the imperfect of credere used here as a past-tense mental verb 'I thought/assumed'; trovar is the poetic infinitive meaning 'to find' (trovar you = 'to find you'); là giù di sotto is a compounded locative phrase ('down there/below') with poetic redundancy (là + giù + sotto) for emphasis. The imperfect credea + infinitive trovar expresses a past expectation.
segments.purgatorio-23-g90: Dove introduces a relative/adverbial clause 'where'; tempo per tempo is an idiomatic repetition meaning 'from time to time' or 'now and then' (tempo + per + tempo), si ristora is a present-tense reflexive verb form (ristorare with si) meaning 'is refreshed' or 'recovers itself' and functions impersonally here ('one is refreshed'/'people refresh themselves'). Overall the clause explains the place where the speaker expected to find the other: a place where people are refreshed at intervals.
segments.purgatorio-23-g91: 'Ond'' is a contracted, poetic discourse connector (from onde/onde) meaning 'whereupon' or 'therefore'; 'elli' is an archaic/metrical form of 'egli' (he) used as the speaker of the reported speech; 'a me' is the indirect object 'to me', marking the addressee of the reported speech.
segments.purgatorio-23-g92: Sì tosto = 'so soon/so quickly' (adverb + adverb). The clitic 'm' (m' ha) = mi + avere (present perfect auxiliary) gives 'has led me' with past participle 'condotto' placed after the auxiliary for metrical ordering. 'a ber' is the infinitive construction 'to drink' (archaic infinitive 'ber' = modern 'bere'). 'lo dolce assenzo d'i martìri' = 'the sweet assenzio/ wormwood of martyrdom' (assenzio used poetically); 'la Nella mia' places the feminine proper name with the article (la) and the possessive after the name ('mia'), a common Tuscan/poetic order meaning 'my Nella' and functioning as the feminine subject previously implied; 'con suo pianger dirotto' = 'with her unrestrained/torrential weeping', where 'suo' agrees with Nella and 'dirotto' qualifies the manner of 'pianger'.
segments.purgatorio-23-g93: 'Con suoi prieghi devoti e con sospiri' = instrumentals 'with her devout prayers and with sighs'; 'tratto m'ha' is an inversion of 'mi ha tratto' (perfect tense of 'trarre'), with past participle 'tratto' before the auxiliary for metrical effect and the clitic 'm'' attached to 'ha' (has drawn me). 'de la costa' uses archaic 'de' = 'da' meaning 'from the slope/coast'; 'ove s'aspetta' is a relative clause: 'ove' = where, and 's'aspetta' = impersonal 'si aspetta' ('one waits' / 'is awaited'), the reflexive/clitic 's' marking the impersonal construction.
segments.purgatorio-23-g94: The conjunction 'e' links the second clause. 'Liberato m'ha' = 'has freed me' (past participle before auxiliary again for meter; clitic attached to auxiliary). 'de li altri giri' = 'from the other circuits/rounds' with archaic plural article 'li' and 'giri' meaning turns/circles (the other terraces/rounds of Purgatory).
segments.purgatorio-23-g1: "Mentre che" introduces a temporal clause meaning "while." The verb phrase "ficcava ïo" uses the imperfect (ficcava) with the subject pronoun ïo placed after the verb for poetic word order, giving "I was fixing/fastening (my) eyes." "Per la fronda verde" is a prepositional phrase "through the green foliage." The simile beginning "sì come far suole chi...perde" inverts the usual order (suole fare) to the poetic "far suole" and means "as one is wont to do," with "chi ... perde" a relative clause in the present indicative "who loses."
segments.purgatorio-23-g2: "Lo più che" here functions adverbially as "most/ofttimes" or "more than that," followed by the imperfect "mi dicea" (he used to say to me). Word order is straightforward: the subject "padre" precedes the verb and the indirect object "mi."
segments.purgatorio-23-g3: "Figliuole" is a vocative plural (archaic spelling) addressing the daughters/maidens; no verb is required here because it is direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-23-g4: "Vienne" is an archaic/poetic form of the verb "venire" used exhortatively here (comparable to an imperative/subjunctive) meaning "come." "Oramai" is an adverb "now/at this point."
segments.purgatorio-23-g5: "Ché" = "for/because." "’l tempo che n’è imposto" is a relative clause: "the time that has been imposed on us/that is imposed (n' = ne/of it or of us)," with è (present) + imposto (past participle). "Più utilmente" is an adverbial phrase "more usefully/advantageously." "Compartir si vuole" uses an impersonal/passive construction with "si" + present "vuole" meaning "it is wished/required that the sharing/division take place" (compartir as infinitive, archaic form).
segments.purgatorio-23-g6: "Io volsi ’l viso" is simple past (volsi, passato remoto) with the subject pronoun "Io" emphasized by poetic placement after the verb; "’l viso" is the direct object "the face" (i.e., 'I turned my face').
segments.purgatorio-23-g7: "E ’l passo non men tosto" parallels the previous clause: "and the step/pace no less quickly." "Non men" = "not less" (emphatic), and "tosto" = "soon/quickly." Word order places the noun phrase after the conjunction for balance with the previous clause.
segments.purgatorio-23-g8: "Appresso i savi" is a prepositional phrase meaning "near/close to the sages." "Appresso" here = "up to/near," and "i savi" is the plural nominative "the wise men/sages."
segments.purgatorio-23-g9: The relative clause "che parlavan sìe" uses the imperfect "parlavan" (they were speaking). "Sìe" is an archaic particle: in context it modifies the manner of speaking (roughly "thus/there/so"), a poetic form not identical to modern forms but functioning adverbially.
segments.purgatorio-23-g10: "Che l’andar mi facean di nullo costo" is another relative/result clause: "that their walking made my going cost me nothing." "L’andar" is a nominalized infinitive "the going/pace," "mi facean" (imperfect) = "they made/caused for me," and "di nullo costo" = "of no cost/without effort."
segments.purgatorio-23-g11: "Ed ecco" = "and behold." The verbs "piangere e cantar s’udìe" are in a passive/impersonal past construction: "weeping and singing were heard" (s’udìe = archaic impersonal form of "si udìa" / "one heard" or "was heard"). "Labïa mëa" is a Latin phrase (Labia mea = 'my lips') incorporated as the words heard; the diaeresis on ï signals the syllable break.
segments.purgatorio-23-g12: "Domine" is a vocative Latin address "O Lord." "Per modo tal" is a phrase meaning "in such a manner/way" with "per" + "modo" and the demonstrative/adverb "tal" (such).
segments.purgatorio-23-g13: "Che" introduces the result clause "that..."; "diletto e doglia parturìe" literally uses the verb-form "parturìe" (poetic/archaic) meaning "would bring forth/give birth to." Grammatically, the plural nouns "diletto e doglia" are the things being brought forth: "delight and pain would be born/produced."
segments.purgatorio-23-g14: "O dolce padre" is a direct vocative address: "O/Oh sweet father." The interjection "O" (or "Oh") plus the adjective and noun form a standard apostrophe.
segments.purgatorio-23-g15: "Che è quel ch’i’ odo?" is an interrogative: "what is that which I hear?" "Che è quel" = "what/which is that," while "ch’i’" is the contracted form of "che io" (that I) and "odo" is the present "I hear."
segments.purgatorio-23-g95: Tanto begins a correlative comparison (tanto...quanto) meaning 'so/no matter how much'. The clause è a Dio più cara e più diletta is a predicate with indirect object a Dio ('to God' / 'in God's eyes'), using two parallel comparative adjectives (più cara, più diletta). The subject la vedovella mia (diminutive 'my little widow') follows the predicate for poetic word order; mia is postpositive.
segments.purgatorio-23-g96: Che is a relative pronoun referring back to la vedovella mia ('whom'). Molto is an adverb modifying the verb, and amai is the passato remoto 'I loved' (first-person singular), so the whole short clause says 'whom I loved very much.'
segments.purgatorio-23-g97: Quanto here pairs with the opening tanto and functions as 'to the extent that' / 'in proportion as.' In bene operare is an infinitival phrase with operare used as a noun (the act of doing good) governed by the preposition in. È più soletta is the copula + comparative adjective: soletta (feminine) = 'more solitary/solely devoted,' describing the widow's greater devotion to good works.
segments.purgatorio-23-g98: Ché introduces the reason ('for' / 'because'). La Barbagia di Sardigna is the subject (the region of Barbagia in Sardinia). Assai is an intensifier ('much'); ne le femmine sue = 'in its women' where ne is an adverbial/partitive pronoun meaning 'in/of them.' Più è pudica is a comparative predicate ('is more chaste'), and the comparison completes with che + la Barbagia dov’ io la lasciai ('than the Barbagia where I left her'), with dov' = dove and la the object 'her.'
segments.purgatorio-23-g99: O is the vocative interjection ('O' / 'oh') addressing someone; dolce is an adjective 'dear/sweet' placed before the noun for emphasis; frate ('brother') is a vocative noun (can mean 'brother' or 'friar' depending on context).
segments.purgatorio-23-g100: Che introduces the interrogative clause ('what'). Vuo' is an archaic contraction of vuoi (second-person singular present of volere, 'do you want'); tu is the stressed subject pronoun. Ch’io = che io ('that I') leads a subjunctive clause, and dica is present subjunctive (from dire) used after volere: literally 'what do you want that I say?' = 'what would you have me say?'.
segments.purgatorio-23-g101: 'Tempo futuro' = 'future time' (noun + adjective). 'm'' = 'mi � � ' (mi + è) functions as 'is to me' or 'lies before me'; 'già' = already; 'nel cospetto' = 'in sight/presence' i.e. 'before my eyes'. Word order places the clitic with the verb (m'è) as is normal in Italian.
segments.purgatorio-23-g102: 'Cui' is a relative pronoun referring to the preceding 'tempo futuro' (meaning 'which' or 'to which'); 'non sarà' is future tense 'will not be'; 'quest' ora' = 'this hour'; 'molto antica' literally 'very ancient/old' is used idiomatically to mean 'long in the past' or here 'very long (a time)', so the clause means this hour will not be long before that future time — i.e., it will happen soon.
segments.purgatorio-23-g103: Structure: 'nel qual' = 'in which' (introducing the circumstance). 'sarà in pergamo interdetto' = 'will be forbidden in/by a parchment (i.e. by a written decree on parchment)' — 'in pergamo' indicates the medium of the decree (pergamo = parchment) and 'interdetto' is passive/predicative 'prohibited'. The indirect object 'a le sfacciate donne fiorentine' = 'to the shameless Florentine women' marks who is forbidden, while the delayed nominal phrase 'l'andar mostrando con le poppe il petto' ('the going about showing, with the breasts, the chest') is the thing forbidden: an infinitive used as a noun ('l'andar mostrando...' = 'going about showing ...'). 'Con le poppe il petto' specifies that they show the chest by means of their breasts.
segments.purgatorio-23-g104: Quai is an exclamatory/interrogative plural 'what' (literally 'which kind of'); barbare here is feminine plural and functions as a noun ('barbarian women'). Fuor is an archaic/dialectal past form meaning 'were' (Dante often shortens past forms); mai means 'ever' and intensifies the rhetorical question. The word order places the exclamation before the verb for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-23-g105: Quai repeats the exclamatory 'what/which' and saracine is feminine plural (Saracen women). The comma shows the appositive pause: the speaker lists another class of women in parallel to 'barbare.'
segments.purgatorio-23-g106: Cui is a dative relative ('to whom' / 'for whom') linking these women to the following impersonal verb. Bisognasse is an old-form verb used here with a potential/conditional sense ('would be necessary' / 'would need'); bisognare is typically impersonal, so the construction means 'for whom it would be necessary' or 'who would need.'
segments.purgatorio-23-g107: Per + infinitive expresses purpose ('in order to'). Farle is fare + clitic le ('to make them' — le = them, feminine plural); the clitic is attached to the infinitive. Ir is an archaic infinitive form of 'ire'/'andare' meaning 'to go.' Coperte is an adjective (past participle) agreeing with le and means 'covered' or 'veiled' — so 'farle ir coperte' = 'to make them go about covered/veiled.'
segments.purgatorio-23-g108: The correlative o ... o means 'either ... or.' Spirituali is an adjective used substantively here ('spiritual [disciplines/practices]') and altre discipline is 'other disciplines/practices.' The whole phrase asks whether spiritual or other kinds of disciplines would be necessary to make such women go veiled.
segments.purgatorio-23-g32: Io dicea fra me stesso pensando: — 'Io' is the subject pronoun 'I'. 'Dicea' is the imperfect tense of dire (3rd person or 1st person singular in older spelling) used here as a first-person imperfect (‘I was saying’), giving an ongoing past action. 'Fra me stesso' is a reflexive locution meaning 'within myself' or 'to myself' and 'pensando' is a gerund ('thinking') that comments on the mental action; the colon announces the direct speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-23-g33: 'Ecco' is an exclamatory particle meaning 'behold' or 'here is'. 'La gente' is the noun phrase 'the people' (subject). 'Che' introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). 'Perdé' is a past tense (remote/past) of perdere — 'lost'. 'Ierusalemme' is the proper noun 'Jerusalem'. Word order is straightforward: exclamation + subject + relative clause (who lost Jerusalem).
segments.purgatorio-23-g34: 'Quando' begins a temporal clause ('when'). 'Maria' is the proper name 'Mary'. 'Nel' is the contraction of 'in + il' ('in the' or 'at the'); here it introduces the place/target 'in/at the son' (i.e., toward the son). 'Figlio' = 'son'. 'Diè' is the passato remoto (third person singular of dare) meaning 'gave'. The phrase 'diè di becco' is idiomatic/poetic: 'di becco' involves 'becco' (beak) and together it reads as 'gave a peck' or 'gave (by) the beak'; the whole is an exclamatory, colloquial image. Note the strong exclamation mark closing the clause.
segments.purgatorio-23-g35: 'Parean' is the imperfect (3rd person plural) of parere meaning 'they seemed' or 'they appeared' and agrees with 'l'occhiaie' (plural). 'L’occhiaie' are the dark circles or hollows around the eyes. 'Anella' is used poetically to express 'ringed' or 'like a ring' (here functioning as a predicate image), and 'sanza' is the archaic form of 'senza' meaning 'without'. 'Gemme' are 'gems' or 'jewels'. Together the metaphor says the eye-sockets looked like a ring lacking jewels.
segments.purgatorio-23-g36: 'Chi' = 'whoever'. 'Nel viso de li uomini' = 'in the face of men' (literally 'in the faces of men'); note the older preposition 'de' for 'di' and 'li' for the article 'the'. 'Legge' is present tense of leggere used in the sense 'reads/interprets' (you 'read' a sign). '"Omo"' is the word 'man' being read; the following clause 'ben avria quivi conosciuta l'emme' uses 'ben' as intensifier 'well' and 'avria' (archaic conditional of avere, modern 'avrebbe') plus past participle 'conosciuta' — 'would there have recognized' — with 'l'emme' meaning the letter M. So the structure: whoever reads 'man' on the faces of men would there have recognized the letter M.
segments.purgatorio-23-g37: 'Chi crederebbe' is a conditional interrogative: 'Who would believe...'. 'Che' introduces the subordinate clause. 'L'odor d’un pomo' = 'the smell/scent of an apple'. 'Sì governasse' uses the imperfect subjunctive (governasse) after the conditional 'crederebbe' — a classical sequence of moods — meaning 'could/should so govern' or 'could so control'. The adverb 'sì' = 'so'.
segments.purgatorio-23-g38: 'Generando' is a gerund meaning 'generating' or 'producing', and 'brama' is a noun meaning 'longing' or 'desire' (here the effect produced by the scent). The comma separates the consequence (producing desire) from what follows.
segments.purgatorio-23-g39: 'E' = 'and'. 'Quel d’un’acqua' = 'that (scent) of a water' — 'quel' points back to 'odor' (that scent). The short phrase isolates the parallel example (a scent of water) to be compared with the apple's scent.
segments.purgatorio-23-g40: 'Non sappiendo como?' is a rhetorical question: 'not knowing how?' 'Non' negates; 'sappiendo' is a gerund of sapere ('knowing'); 'como' (archaic form of 'come') means 'how'. The whole asks incredulously that this should happen without knowing how.
segments.purgatorio-23-g41: 'Già era in ammirar' is an impersonal or semi-impersonal phrase: 'I (or one) was already in admiration / was already marveling'. 'Era' is the imperfect of essere ('was'), and 'ammirar' is the infinitive 'to marvel/admire' used nominally. 'Che sì li affama' is a subordinate clause introduced by 'che' ('that'), with 'sì' = 'so' and 'li' (here older form for 'them') the object pronoun; 'affama' (present tense) means 'starves' or 'makes famished', so 'that so starves them'.
segments.purgatorio-23-g42: 'Per la cagione ancor non manifesta' = 'because of the cause still not manifest' — 'per' = 'because of/through', 'la cagione' = 'the cause', 'ancor' = 'ancora' = 'still', and 'non manifesta' = 'not revealed/clear'. 'Di lor magrezza e di lor trista squama' gives the matter of that cause: 'of their thinness (emaciation) and of their sad/woeful skin/scaling' — 'lor' is the archaic possessive 'loro'.
segments.purgatorio-23-g43: 'Ed ecco' = 'and behold'. 'Del profondo de la testa' = 'from the depths of the head' ('del' = di + il, 'de' = di). 'Volse' is the passato remoto of volgere (here 'turned' or 'directed'), and the clause 'volse a me li occhi un'ombra' has poetic word order: literally 'a shadow turned the eyes to me' (English would place the subject 'a shadow' before the verb; Dante places 'li occhi' earlier for emphasis). 'E guardò fiso' = 'and (it/he) looked fixedly' — 'guardò' past tense, 'fiso' = 'fixed/intently'.
segments.purgatorio-23-g44: 'Poi' = 'then/afterwards'. 'Gridò' is the passato remoto of gridare ('he shouted/cried out'). 'Forte' functions here as an adverb meaning 'loudly' (modern Italian would use 'a voce forte' or simply interpret as 'shouted loudly'). The colon introduces the direct question that follows.
segments.purgatorio-23-g45: 'Qual' is the interrogative 'what/which'. 'Grazia' in this context means 'favor' or 'grace' (often used idiomatically as 'what kindness/favor is this?' or 'what trick is this?'). 'M’ è' is the contracted form of 'mi è' ('is to me' / 'is for me'), so the whole question asks 'What favor/grace is this to me?' — a startled, rhetorical complaint.
segments.purgatorio-23-g59: This segment is only a punctuation mark (a full stop/period) marking the end of the preceding speech; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-23-g60: A simple noun phrase in the expected order article + noun + possessive: 'La' (definite article) + 'faccia' (noun 'face') + 'tua' (possessive adjective 'your'). In Italian the possessive commonly follows the noun and agrees in gender/number with it (here feminine singular).
segments.purgatorio-23-g61: An embedded relative clause introduced by the contracted 'ch’' (= che, 'that/which'). 'io lagrimai' is passato remoto (1st person singular) 'I wept' (an emphatic past used frequently in poetry). 'già' = 'already/once'. 'morta' is an adjectival past participle ('dead') referring back to the (feminine) person whose face it is; the clause is elliptical (omitting an explicit verb like 'era' — i.e. 'when she was already dead').
segments.purgatorio-23-g62: 'mi dà di pianger' is idiomatic: 'dare di' + infinitive here = 'to give/produce the impulse to (do something)' so 'it makes me weep' or 'gives me cause to weep.' 'mo' (poetic/archaic) = 'now'. 'non minor doglia' literally 'not smaller pain' = 'no less grief'; the adjective 'minor' is used comparatively but negated to mean 'not less'.
segments.purgatorio-23-g63: Elliptical, poetic word order: the verb 'rispuos'' is an archaic/poetic past form 'rispuose/rispose' (passato remoto 'replied'); it appears before the subject 'io' and the indirect object 'lui' for metrical reasons. The apostrophe marks the elision of a final vowel.
segments.purgatorio-23-g64: 'Veggendo' is a present participle/gerund of an archaic form of 'vedere' ('seeing'), here with the clitic object '-la' attached (veggendola = 'seeing her'). 'sì' is an adverb 'so/thus'. 'torta' is an adjective (feminine) describing the face: literally 'twisted' or 'distorted' (from 'tortare'), used here in the sense 'so distorted'.
segments.purgatorio-23-g65: 'Però' = 'therefore/then'. 'mi dì' is an imperative 'dì' (tell) with the indirect object 'mi' ('to me'); the object is placed before the verb here for emphasis/metrical inversion (more usual modern form would be 'dimmi').
segments.purgatorio-23-g66: A short oath 'per Dio' = 'by God' or 'for God's sake' strengthening the petition; 'per' = by/for, 'Dio' = God.
segments.purgatorio-23-g67: 'che' here introduces the content of the request and functions like 'what/why' ('what causes/what is it that...'). 'sì' = 'so'. 'vi' is the clitic pronoun referring to 'you' (or 'yourselves' / 'in you' depending on context) and 'sfoglia' is 3rd-person singular present indicative of 'sfogliare' used in the sense 'to strip/peel/expose' — so the phrase asks 'what makes you so stripped/exposed.'
segments.purgatorio-23-g68: 'non mi far dir' = 'do not make me say' (fare + infinitive = causative construction: 'to make [someone] do [something]'). 'mentr’' is the contracted 'mentre' ('while'); 'io mi maraviglio' = 'I marvel/am astonished' (present tense), hence 'don't make me speak while I'm amazed.' The apostrophe in 'mentr’' marks elision for meter.
segments.purgatorio-23-g69: 'ché' = 'for/because'. The sequence 'mal può dir' uses 'mal' (adverb 'badly/scarcely') to weaken 'può' so the sense is 'can scarcely speak' or 'hardly can say.' 'chi è pien d’altra voglia' = 'he who is full of another desire' (i.e., occupied by some other longing), which explains why he can hardly speak.
segments.purgatorio-23-g70: 'Ed' = 'and' (conjunction); 'elli' is an archaic/poetic subject pronoun meaning 'he' (here introducing direct speech). 'a me' = 'to me' (indirect object) — the entire segment sets the speaker of the reported speech: 'And he to me:'.
segments.purgatorio-23-g71: Long reported clause: 'De l’etterno consiglio' = 'from the eternal counsel' (De + l' = from the). 'cade' is 3rd-singular present of 'cadere' = 'falls' (used impersonally of a power descending). 'vertù' = 'virtue/power' (what falls). 'ne l’acqua e ne la pianta' = 'into the water and into the plant' (ne = 'in/into' here). 'rimasa' (past participle of 'rimanere', archaic spelling) = 'left/remained' agreeing with 'pianta' (feminine): 'the plant left behind'. 'ond’' = 'onde/whence/from which' linking cause to effect, and 'io sì m’assottiglio' = reflexive present 'I thus become thin / I am thereby diminished' (m’ = mi, reflexive; assottiglio = I thin). Overall: 'By the eternal counsel, virtue falls into the water and into the plant left behind, from which I thus grow thin.'
segments.purgatorio-23-g72: Tutta esta gente = an emphatic noun phrase 'all these people' (esta is an archaic/poetic form of questa). The relative clause che piangendo canta places the gerund piangendo ('weeping') before the finite verb canta ('sings'), a typical poetic order meaning 'who sing while weeping.' Per + infinitive (per seguitar) gives the reason/purpose ('for having followed' / 'because they followed'); gola is used figuratively for gluttony ('the throat' = gluttony) and oltra misura is a postpositive adverbial phrase 'beyond measure.'
segments.purgatorio-23-g73: In fame e ’n sete = 'in hunger and in thirst' (’n is the contracted form of in). Qui si rifà santa uses si as a reflexive/passive marker; rifà (from rifare) in the present indicative (3rd sg.) with si means 'is made/renewed' or 'is restored,' so 'is made holy here.' Santa agrees with gente taken as a collective feminine noun.
segments.purgatorio-23-g74: Di bere e di mangiar fronted expresses the objects ('a desire for drinking and for eating'); the verb phrase n’accende cura is delayed and inverted: l’odor (the scent) is the grammatical subject that 'kindles care/desire' (accende cura) in us. N’ is the contraction of ne ('of it' / 'from it') referring back to the scent; accende is present indicative 3rd sg.; cura here means 'care/urge' (i.e. desire). The relative clause ch’esce del pomo e de lo sprazzo modifies odor, and che si distende su per sua verdura describes the sprazzo: che + si distende (reflexive/spreading) + su/per sua verdura = 'which stretches up through its foliage.' Note de lo is an archaic form of dello.
segments.purgatorio-23-g75: E non pur una volta = 'and not only once' (non pur = 'not even/not only' with pur = an emphatic/poetic 'even'). Volta here means 'time/occasion.'
segments.purgatorio-23-g76: questo spazzo girando = 'this space/spot, turning' where spazzo (a cleared area/space) is the subject and girando (gerund of girare) adds the sense 'as it turns' or 'as it revolves'; the participial/gerundial phrase is placed after the noun in a poetic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-23-g77: si rinfresca nostra pena = 'our suffering is refreshed' with si marking the reflexive/passive; rinfresca (from rinfrere/rinfrescare) is present indicative 3rd sg. and nostra pena is the affected noun phrase. The colon that follows prepares the speaker's comment.
segments.purgatorio-23-g79: e dovria dir sollazzo = 'and I ought to say
segments.purgatorio-23-g80: ché quella voglia a li alberi ci mena = 'for that desire leads us to the trees' (ché = because). Voglia is a longing/desire; a li alberi 'to the trees' is the direction/goal; ci = 'to us/us' (here 'leads us'); mena is present indicative 'leads.' The following relative clause che menò Cristo lieto a dire 'Elì' uses menò (past of menare) 'led' and places lieto + a + infinitive (lieto a dire) to mean 'moved/led Christ, rejoicing, to say
segments.purgatorio-23-g81: quando ne liberò con la sua vena = 'when he freed us from them with his vein' (quando = when). Liberò is passato remoto (3rd sg.) 'freed/redeemed'; ne is the pronoun referring to 'from them' (freed us from those things), con la sua vena is a figurative phrase 'with his vein' meaning 'by his blood' (vena = vein). The clause gives the temporal/causal reference for Christ's action.
segments.purgatorio-23-g82: E io a lui: = 'And I to him:' a short reported-speech lead-in; io is emphatic 'I' and a lui marks the indirect object 'to him.'
segments.purgatorio-23-g83: «Forese, = an archaic form of forse meaning 'perhaps' or 'maybe'; used here as the speaker's hedging particle before his conjecture.
segments.purgatorio-23-g84: da quel dì nel qual mutasti mondo a miglior vita = 'from that day in which you changed [your] worldly life to a better life.' Da quel dì introduces the temporal phrase; nel qual is a literary relative 'in which' referring to quel dì; mutasti is 2nd person singular past (you changed) — an archaic/poetic past form; mondo here is used in a figurative sense 'world/life' and a miglior vita = 'to a better life' (miglior = better).
segments.purgatorio-23-g85: cinqu’ anni non son vòlti infino a qui = 'five years have not yet rolled by up to here.' Cinqu’ is the elided form of cinque (apostrophe marks elision), son vòlti is a present-tense auxiliary (sono + past participle vòlti) used to mean 'have turned/elapsed' (archaic syntax), and infino a qui = 'up to here/up to now.'
segments.purgatorio-23-g109: Ma ('but') starts a contrast. se is the conditional 'if' (archaic spelling kept); le svergognate is a feminine plural noun phrase 'the shameless ones' serving as the subject; fosser is an archaic subjunctive/imperfect plural (modern fosssero) meaning 'were' or 'might be'; certe is an adjective 'certain (of)'. di quel che introduces a relative content clause 'of what/that which', and 'l ciel veloce loro ammanna' places the agent ('the swift heaven') before the verb ammanna ('bestows/assigns to them'), with loro marking the indirect object.
segments.purgatorio-23-g110: già ('already' / 'by then') frames the next result clause. per urlare functions as a purposive phrase 'to shout/for shouting'. avrian is a poetic contraction of the conditional 'avrebbero' (they would have), used here in a periphrastic construction with an adjective: avrian le bocche aperte = 'they would have their mouths open.' aperte is the past participle used adjectivally.
segments.purgatorio-23-g111: ché is a causal conjunction 'for/because', introducing the explanation that follows; it links the result just stated to the reason that the speaker will give.
segments.purgatorio-23-g112: This is a conditional clause: se ('if') + subject l’antiveder ('the foresight' or 'prevision') + qui ('here/in this matter') + non m’inganna ('does not deceive me'). Antiveder is a noun formed from anticipating sight; inganna is present indicative 'deceives' with clitic m' = 'me'.
segments.purgatorio-23-g113: prima here means 'before/earlier' (indicating temporal precedence). fien is an archaic verb form (medieval/poetic) used for a third-person plural copula or periphrastic future — functionally 'they will be' (compare modern saranno/saranno); triste is predicate adjective 'sad'. che introduces the clause that follows; in this construction che has a comparative/temporal sense 'before/than'. le guance is the noun phrase 'the cheeks'. impeli is an archaic form of impellere/impellere (3rd person) 'impels, urges' and colui che mo si consola con nanna is a relative clause 'he who now consoles himself with sleep', where si consola is reflexive 'comforts himself' and nanna = 'sleep/nap'.
segments.purgatorio-23-g114: Deh is an interjection (entreaty) meaning 'ah' or 'pray'—an appeal to the addressee.
segments.purgatorio-23-g115: frate is a vocative 'brother' (addressing a religious brother or simply 'brother'), used directly to call the addressee.
segments.purgatorio-23-g116: or = 'now'; fa is an imperative 2nd-person singular of fare ('do/make') used idiomatically in the construction fa che + subjunctive/indicative meaning 'do that/see to it that'. che introduces the subordinate clause; più non = 'no longer'; mi ti celi is a reflexive-style verb phrase where celi (from celare) means 'you hide yourself' and the clitic mi indicates the action is 'from me' (i.e. 'stop hiding yourself from me'). The unusual order mi ti celi simply places clitics before the verb, as in older/poetic Italian.
segments.purgatorio-23-g117: vedi is an imperative or present 2nd-person form meaning 'see/notice' (addressing the brother). che introduces a content clause 'that/that not only I...'. non pur io = 'not only I' (pur = archaic 'even/only' used here in the sense 'only/merely').
segments.purgatorio-23-g118: ma = 'but'; questa gente tutta = 'all these people' (questa modifies gente, tutta intensifies 'all'); rimira is present indicative 'gaze at/watch' (re- + mira); là dove = 'there where'; ’l is the definite article 'the'; sol here in the text is poetically used for 'sole' (the sun) though the supplied sense-list gives only 'alone/only' — context makes it 'the sun'; veli is a verb form (present indicative/subjunctive of velare) 'veils' or 'is veiling' (i.e. 'where the sun veils/sets').
segments.purgatorio-23-g119: Per ch’io a lui is an introductory formula: per che (contracted) = 'wherefore/so' and io a lui = 'I to him' marks reported speech ('so I said to him'). The clitic ch’ stands for che; word order places the speaker and addressee before the quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-23-g120: Se tu riduci a mente = 'If you bring back to mind/recall' — se introduces a conditional; tu riduci is present indicative (you bring) used to ask the addressee to recall; a mente is an idiom 'to mind'. qual fosti meco = 'what sort of person you were with me' or 'what you were when you were with me'; fosti is an older perfect-past form ('you were'), and meco = 'with me' (archaic clitic form).
segments.purgatorio-23-g121: e qual io teco fui = 'and what I was with you' — e = 'and'; qual = 'what/which'; io = 'I'; teco (archaic) = 'with you' (note: the provided lemma list is misleading); fui is first-person past ('I was'). The parallel clause balances the earlier 'qual fosti meco'.
segments.purgatorio-23-g122: ancor = 'still/yet'; fia is an archaic/fixed-form future ('will be') or subjunctive used futurally (modern sarà); grave = 'heavy/burdensome' (metaphorically 'painful'); il memorar presente = 'the remembrance/recalling at present' (memorar is an infinitive used as a noun 'remembering', modified by presente). The clause states that the memory will continue to be grievous.
segments.purgatorio-23-g123: Di quella vita is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from/of that life' (here: 'away from that life') and precedes the verb for emphasis. Mi volse is an archaic past form (volse) used transitively with the indirect object mi (me) to mean 'turned me' or 'turned me away'; costui is the subject 'this man'. The relative clause che mi va innanzi follows and modifies costui: che = 'who' and mi va innanzi = 'goes before me' (va innanzi = 'goes ahead').
segments.purgatorio-23-g124: L’altr’ ier is a compressed, poetic way to say 'l'altro ier' = 'the other day' / 'yesterday'; l' is the article, altr- is 'other/another', and ier = 'yesterday' or 'the other day'. The whole phrase is a temporal adverbial modifying the preceding clause.
segments.purgatorio-23-g125: Quando introduces a temporal clause 'when'. Tonda here is an adjectival/adverbial use meaning something like 'complete' or 'in full' and modifies the verb phrase that follows. Vi is a locative 'there'; si mostrò is reflexive/passive-seemingly intransitive 'appeared/was shown itself', with la suora di colui (the sister/nun of that man) as the grammatical subject placed after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-23-g126: E = 'and'. 'L sol' is written elliptically; l = the article, sol is ambiguous in the supplied lemma list but here functions as a nominal element related to what was shown, and mostrai is the first‑person singular past tense 'I showed' (mostrai). The clause is a short, emphatic statement ('and I showed it/the sun').
segments.purgatorio-23-g127: Costui is the subject 'this one'; per la profonda notte is an adverbial phrase 'through the deep night'. Menato m'ha is a compound past (past participle menato + ha) meaning 'has led me' (note the inverted order menato m'ha). D'i veri morti = 'out of/from the true dead' (d' = di). Con questa vera carne introduces the accompaniment/instrument: 'with this true flesh/body'. Che 'l seconda is a relative clause: che = 'which/that' and seconda is an archaic usage (here a verbal sense) meaning 'follows/accompanies him' — syntactically the relative modifies 'carne' and '’l' = lo refers back to the person mentioned.
segments.purgatorio-23-g128: Indi = 'then' (or 'thence'); m'han tratto sù is a compound past (hanno tratto) meaning 'have drawn/pulled me up' with sù 'upwards'. Li suoi conforti is the plural noun phrase 'his comforts/consolations' and functions as the agent of the verb even though it follows for style (inversion).
segments.purgatorio-23-g129: Salendo e rigirando are present participles ('ascending and winding/turning again') that describe the motion; la montagna is the direct object ('the mountain') and che introduces a relative clause modifying it. Drizza voi = 'straightens/sets you upright' (drizza = present indicative 'makes straight'), and the following che introduces a nested relative clause 'that the world made crooked' (che 'l mondo fece torti) where fece is past of fare and torti = 'crooked/wrong' (poetic). The sentence stacks two relative clauses: the mountain that straightens you (you = voi), you whom the world had made crooked.
segments.purgatorio-23-g130: Tanto dice di farmi sua compagna = 'He says so much about making me his companion': dice di + infinitive (farmi) expresses what he claims/wishes. Che introduces a result or content clause: io sarò là dove fia Beatrice — io sarò is future 'I will be', dove fia is an archaic/future-subjunctive form of essere ('will be') used in relative locating where Beatrice will be.
segments.purgatorio-23-g131: Quivi = 'there'; convien is an impersonal verb meaning 'it is necessary' or 'one must'. The clause introduced by che (that) takes a subjunctive (rimagna, archaic subjunctive of rimanere) — sanza lui = 'without him' indicates that the speaker must remain separated from him. The construction convien che + subjunctive expresses necessity.
segments.purgatorio-23-g132: Virgilio è questi literally 'Virgil is this one' — an identification with somewhat inverted word order (predicate before demonstrative). Che così mi dice is a relative clause 'who speaks to me thus' (che = 'who', così = 'thus', mi dice = 'says to me').
segments.purgatorio-23-g133: E = 'and'. Addita’lo is a contracted past form meaning 'he pointed him out' (additò + lo); the enclitic lo is the direct object 'him/it'. This is a terse narrative clause: 'and he pointed him out.'
segments.purgatorio-23-g134: E quest' altro è quell' ombra = 'and this other is that shade/shadow' (identification). Per cui introduces a relative/causal phrase 'by/through whom'; scosse is a past verb here (poetic past of scuotere = 'shook') — (the supplied sense list is mismatched), dianzi = 'before/previously', ogne pendice = 'every slope', lo vostro regno = 'your kingdom' (lo = the). Word order is poetic: the relative phrase describes the shade's effect on the kingdom in past time.
segments.purgatorio-23-g135: Che = 'which/that' introduces a clause modifying 'regno' (or the whole preceding idea). Da sé = 'of/from itself' or 'by itself'. Lo is the object pronoun referring back to the kingdom, and sgombra is present indicative (sgombrare) 'clears/removes/sets free'. The clause says the shade (or action) clears it from itself — a compact, poetic statement with da sé 'by its own motion/from itself'.

sentences.purgatorio-23-s5: I began; and he: "Shades who pass by, perhaps loosening the knot of their duty."
sentences.purgatorio-23-s6: Just as thoughtful pilgrims do: when, arriving along the road, they meet unfamiliar people, who turn to them and do not stay, so, behind us — moving rather more quickly — a silent and devout crowd of souls came, passing by and marveling at us.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s7: In the eyes each one was dark and hollow, pale in the face, and so thin that the skin took form from the bones.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s8: I do not believe that Erysichthon was made so dry down to the skin by fasting, when he most feared it.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s14: I would never have recognized him by his face; but in his voice it became plain to me what his look had already won for itself.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s15: This little spark wholly rekindled in me my recognition by the altered countenance, and I recognized Forese’s face.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s16: “Ah, do not struggle with the dry scab that discolours me,” he begged, “nor on account of any lack of flesh that I may have; but tell me the truth about you, say who those two souls are that escort you there; do not go away without speaking to me!”
sentences.purgatorio-23-s25: If, before now, the power in you to sin any more had been ended — so that the hour of that salutary sorrow which makes one return to God might occur — how then have you come up here again?
sentences.purgatorio-23-s26: I thought I would find you down there, where one is refreshed from time to time.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s27: Whereupon he said to me: "She has so quickly led me to drink the sweet wormwood of martyrdom — my Nella — with her torrent of weeping."
sentences.purgatorio-23-s28: With her devout prayers and with sighs she has drawn me from the slope where one waits, and has freed me from the other rounds.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s1: While I fixed my eyes through the green foliage, just as one is wont to do who loses his life following little birds, my father said to me more than once: "Daughters, come now, for the time that has been assigned to us wishes to be divided in a more useful way."
sentences.purgatorio-23-s2: I turned my face, and my step no less quickly, up to the wise men who were talking there, so that their walking made my going cost me nothing.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s3: And behold, one heard weeping and singing, "Labïa mëa, Domine," in such a manner that delight and pain would be brought forth.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s4: "O sweet father, what is that which I hear?"
sentences.purgatorio-23-s29: My little widow — whom I loved very much — is all the dearer and more beloved to God, in proportion as she is more given to doing good; for the Barbagia of Sardinia is much more chaste in its women than the Barbagia where I left her.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s30: O sweet brother, what would you have me say?
sentences.purgatorio-23-s31: Future time already lies before me; it will not be long until this hour, in which, by a parchment decree, it will be forbidden for the shameless Florentine women to go about showing their breasts on their chests.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s32: What barbarian women, what Saracen women, would ever be such that they would need, in order to make them go about veiled, spiritual or other disciplines?
sentences.purgatorio-23-s9: I was saying to myself, thinking: "Behold the people who lost Jerusalem, when Mary gave the son a peck!"
sentences.purgatorio-23-s10: 'Their eye-sockets seemed one ring without gems: whoever reads "man" in the faces of men would well there have recognized the letter M.'
sentences.purgatorio-23-s11: Who would believe that the scent of an apple could so rule, producing desire, and that of a water, not knowing how?
sentences.purgatorio-23-s12: Already I was amazed that it so starved them, because of a cause not yet manifest — of their emaciation and of their sad scaling — and behold, from the deep of the head a shadow turned its eyes to me and looked intently; then he cried loudly: "What favor is this to me?"
sentences.purgatorio-23-s18: “Your face, which I once wept for when (she) was already dead, now gives me cause to weep — no less sorrow,” I replied to him, “seeing it so twisted.”
sentences.purgatorio-23-s19: “Therefore tell me, by God, what makes you so stripped; do not make me speak while I am astonished, for one who is full of another desire can ill speak.”
sentences.purgatorio-23-s20: And he to me: “From the eternal counsel virtue falls into the water and into the plant that was left behind, whence I thus grow thin.”
sentences.purgatorio-23-s21: All these people, who sing while weeping because they followed the throat beyond measure, are made holy here through hunger and thirst.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s22: The scent that comes from the fruit and from the spray which spreads up through its foliage kindles in us a longing to drink and to eat.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s23: And not only once does this space, as it turns, refresh our suffering: I say 'suffering,' and I ought to say 'delight,' for that longing drives us to the trees which once led Christ, rejoicing, to say 'Elì,'
sentences.purgatorio-23-s24: And I to him: 'Perhaps, from that day on which you changed the world to a better life, five years have not yet passed up to here.'
sentences.purgatorio-23-s33: But if the shameless ones were certain of what swift Heaven bestows on them, they would already have their mouths open to cry out; for, if my foresight here does not deceive me, they will be sorrowful before he who now consoles himself with sleep.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s34: Ah, brother, now do that you hide yourself from me no longer!
sentences.purgatorio-23-s35: See that not only I, but all these people are gazing where the sun veils itself.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s36: So I said to him: 'If you call to mind what you were with me, and what I was with you, the recollection will still be grievous.'
sentences.purgatorio-23-s37: This man who goes before me turned me away from that life the other day, when the sister of that man showed herself there in full; and I showed only the sun; “this one has led me through the deep night out of the true dead with this true flesh that follows him.”
sentences.purgatorio-23-s38: Then his consolations have drawn me up, climbing and winding around the mountain that straightens you whom the world made crooked.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s39: He speaks so much of making me his companion that I shall be there where Beatrice will be; there it is necessary that I remain without him.
sentences.purgatorio-23-s40: “This one is Virgil who so speaks to me,” and he pointed him out; “and this other is that shade by whom, earlier, every slope shook your kingdom, which clears it from itself.”
